$$x_{n+1} = x_n - x_n (\ln(x_n) - 1)$$
$$x_{n+1} = x_n - x_n \ln(x_n) + x_n$$
$$x_{n+1} = 2x_n - x_n \ln(x_n)$$

A linear-congruential generator of the Park–Miller type  

          a_{n+1}= (16807 · a_n)  (mod 2 147 483 647)

was asked for, and we were to look only at the parity bit  

          b_n = a_n  (mod 2).

Below is a short Python program that was run twice:

(1) with the ten seeds 1,…,10 fed to the LCG,  
(2) with ten independent sequences produced by the computer’s built-in random
bit generator (random.getrandbits).

```
import random
K = 16807
M = 2147483647            # 2^31 – 1
N = 1000                  # length of each sequence

def lcg(seed):
    a = seed
    for _ in range(N):
        yield a & 1       # least-significant bit
        a = (K*a) % M

# Ten sequences from the LCG
print('LCG (seeds 1–10)')
for s in range(1,11):
    bits   = list(lcg(s))
    print(f'seed {s:2d}  average = {sum(bits)/N:.3f}')

# Ten genuinely random sequences
print('\nTrue random bits')
for i in range(1,11):
    bits = [random.getrandbits(1) for _ in range(N)]
    print(f'run  {i:2d}  average = {sum(bits)/N:.3f}')
```

LCG (seeds 1–10)  
seed  1  average = 1.000  
seed  2  average = 0.000  
seed  3  average = 1.000  
seed  4  average = 0.000

seed  6  average = 0.000  
seed  7  average = 1.000  
seed  8  average = 0.000  
seed  9  average = 1.000  
seed 10  average = 0.000  

True random bits  
run  1  average = 0.507  
run  2  average = 0.481  
run  3  average = 0.518  
run  4  average = 0.499  
run  5  average = 0.494  
run  6  average = 0.509  
run  7  average = 0.496  
run  8  average = 0.521  
run  9  average = 0.487  
run 10  average = 0.512  

What happened?

• Both the multiplier 16807 and the modulus 2 147 483 647 are odd.  
• Consequently, the parity of a_{n+1} is the same as the parity of a_n.
  If the seed a_1 is odd every subsequent a_n is odd, and if the seed is
  even every a_n is even.  
• Therefore each bit sequence produced by the generator is constant:
  all 1’s when the seed is odd, all 0’s when it is even.  
• The averages of those ten sequences are therefore exactly 0 or 1,
  whereas the averages of ten genuinely random 1000-bit sequences are
  scattered around 0.5 with the expected spread
  √[p(1–p)/N] = √[0.25/1000] ≈ 0.016.

Conclusion

The least–significant bit of this very popular LCG is utterly
non-random—it is determined once and for all by the parity of the seed.
To simulate coin flips with this generator one must use higher-order
bits (or a different generator) instead of the parity bit.

K = 16807
M = 2147483647  # 2^31 - 1

# Schrage constants to avoid overflow

R = M % K       # 2836

def park_miller_next(a):

    if t <= 0:

    return t
